content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last week I typed “a black backpack” into a search box and watched a backpack light up yellow inside a 3D reconstruction of my apartment. The backpack wasn’t even visible in the first frame of the video I built the scene from. The pipeline found it anyway, in frames 4 and 5, fused the detections into a single 3D object, and ranked it first for my query.
I want to explain how that works, because what surprised me most is how little machinery it actually takes. And I say that as someone who, six weeks before starting this, barely knew what a Python package was.
The project is called Lumen3D. You give it a casual phone video of a room, and it gives you back an interactive 3D scene in the browser with a text box on top. Type a phrase, “every chair”, “the refrigerator”, “where’s my suitcase”, and the matching objects glow. There’s a live demo on Hugging Face Spaces you can poke at right now. No install, no GPU, no account.
Reconstruction got boring, and that’s the opportunity
Two years ago, turning a video into a 3D model meant COLMAP, camera calibration, and training a NeRF overnight. Then a wave of feed-forward models arrived (DUSt3R, VGGT, π³, and most recently Depth Anything 3) that produce posed 3D geometry from raw images in a single forward pass, no calibration or per-scene training required. Today, video-to-point-cloud is literally one CLI command.
Which creates an awkward situation if you’re trying to build something interesting: the impressive-looking half of the problem is now a solved commodity. Running DA3 on a video and showing people the point cloud proves nothing except that you can install DA3.
So where’s the actual work? Two places, as far as I can tell. First, making the scene mean something: a point cloud is just colored dots; it doesn’t know what a chair is and can’t answer a question. Second, making it usable. Research code that runs once, on the author’s machine, on one specific CUDA version, is not a tool.
There is existing research on language-queryable 3D scenes: LERF, LangSplat, OpenGaussian. But that whole line is built on optimized Gaussian Splatting, which means minutes to hours of per-scene training on serious hardware. Almost nobody had tried doing open-vocabulary querying directly on the cheap feed-forward reconstructions.
That’s the gap Lumen3D sits in. Compared to the LangSplat family, it needs zero per-scene training instead of hours, and a free Colab T4 instead of a high-end GPU. Compared to raw DA3 or VGGT output, it actually understands language and separates individual object instances; the raw models give you geometry and stop there. And it ships as a pip package with a CLI and a web viewer, not as research code.
Nothing in Lumen3D trains or optimizes per scene. The only learned components are three frozen, pretrained foundation models. Everything scene-specific is geometry and bookkeeping, which is exactly why it fits on a free T4 instead of needing a workstation.
The pipeline, in plain terms
Five stages, three models, and no training anywhere:
Video or photos
→ pick sharp, non-redundant frames
→ Depth Anything 3: where is every pixel in 3D?
→ SAM2: which object does every pixel belong to?
→ SigLIP: what does each object mean, as a vector?
→ fuse across frames → one embedding per 3D object instanceQuerying is the easy part: encode the text once, cosine-similarity against the per-object table, rank, highlight. It comes back in under a second because it’s one small matrix multiply.
The mental model that unlocked the whole thing for me: DA3 tells you where each pixel is, and SAM2 tells you what each pixel is. Both operate on the same flat image grid, so depth[y, x] and mask[y, x] describe the same pixel. Once you know a pixel's depth, its camera pose, and which object it belongs to, lifting that object into 3D is arithmetic: pixel to camera ray to world point. I wrote that unprojection myself in plain NumPy and checked it against DA3's internal implementation point-for-point, across 169,344 points. Not because I had to (DA3 has a helper for this) but because I refused to build on a step I couldn't explain.
Two design decisions matter more than everything else.
One embedding per object, not per pixel. The classic failure mode in language-3D work is “feature bleed”: semantics smearing across object boundaries, so querying “mug” softly lights up half the desk. Lumen3D sidesteps this by embedding each masked region as a whole and storing a single vector per object instance. It also collapses memory: a scene ends up being a point cloud plus a table of a few hundred 768-dimensional vectors, rather than a feature attached to every point.
Merging needs two keys, not one. The same chair seen from six angles has to become one chair, and three different chairs must not collapse into a blob. My first version merged detections purely on 3D overlap, and it confidently fused a wooden door with the small trash can sitting next to it. They shared enough voxels, so geometry alone said “same object.” The fix, borrowed from ConceptGraphs, is a dual gate: two detections merge only if they overlap in 3D and their embeddings agree. Location plus meaning. After that change, the door and the trash can went back to being two separate things.
The decision that made it hostable
For a long time my quiet worry was: how does anyone ever see this? The pipeline needs a GPU. I’m not paying for GPU hosting. And “clone the repo and run it on Colab” is where demos go to die.
The answer turned out to be an asymmetry I’d been staring at the whole time: building a scene is heavy, but querying one is nearly free.
Building means running DA3, SAM2, and SigLIP over dozens of frames: minutes of GPU time. But once that’s done, the scene freezes into a bundle: a point cloud and that little embedding table. Answering a query against a frozen bundle needs only SigLIP’s tiny text encoder and a cosine similarity. No GPU, no DA3, no SAM2 anywhere in the process.
So I split the tool along that line. Building is an offline CLI job you run on Colab or your own card. The hosted demo serves pre-built scenes and does nothing but text → vector → rank, which is cheap enough to run on Hugging Face’s free CPU tier indefinitely.
I was paranoid enough about this boundary to write a test for it. It launches a fresh Python interpreter, builds the query server, and asserts that neither depth_anything_3 nor sam2 ever got imported, in an environment where both are installed and could sneak in. The light path stays light because a test will scream if it doesn't.
The whole interface is three commands
bash
lumen3d reconstruct scene.mp4 -o scene/ # heavy, offline, once
lumen3d query scene/ "the red backpack" # instant
lumen3d view scene/ # opens the browser viewerBehind that: a pip-installable package, a Backbone interface so DA3 can be swapped for VGGT or π³ with a config flag, a three.js viewer (the only non-Python piece), a Colab notebook that runs the whole build on a free T4, and 50-odd unit tests.
What actually went wrong
I started this project as a genuine Python beginner: new to virtual environments, shaky on what an embedding even was. I worked through it with an AI tutor under a strict rule: it explains why, I write the code, and it reviews my code the way a senior reviews a junior’s PR. Slow, but everything below is a scar I earned personally.
My tests were green and my code was wrong. The embedding module had 13 passing unit tests. First real GPU run: garbage. The tests had mocked the model call, so they verified my cropping-and-averaging logic while assuming the model boundary was fine. It wasn’t. transformers returns a structured output object, and features[0] was silently grabbing 196 per-patch vectors instead of the one pooled embedding I wanted (features.pooler_output[0]). The lesson I took away: a mock verifies your glue and assumes the boundary, so anything that touches a real model needs at least one real end-to-end run before you believe it. For the rest of the project I marked every unverified model boundary UNVERIFIED in my notes until a GPU run proved it.
My scene rendered upside down. Computer vision counts Y downward from the top-left corner; WebGL counts Y up. One scale(1, -1, 1) and twenty minutes of confusion.
Doubling the points barely improved the scene. Twice the frames, same viewpoints: the new points just landed on surfaces I already had. Density is not coverage. Six frames from different angles beat twelve from the same walk.
The free tier taught me more than the docs did. DA3’s multi-view attention is quadratic in frame count, and a masking detail disables the memory-efficient kernel, so a free T4 runs out of memory the moment you get greedy. I also over-built my first hosted scene: I proudly froze a dense 680-object version, and queries took 19 seconds on the free CPU host, most of it spent serializing an enormous response. On a project like this, the constraints aren’t obstacles to the design; they basically are the design.
And one honest miss. Early on, the query “a wooden door” returned a blank wall, and I filed it away as a segmentation limitation. It was actually an architecture bug: the pipeline only discovered objects in frame 0 and merely tracked them afterward, so anything that appeared mid-video simply didn’t exist. Rebuilding discovery to run on every frame, with 3D association stitching identities together across frames, is what made the late-appearing backpack findable at all. The door query works now too.
Where it stands
The loop works end-to-end and I’ve stopped being surprised by it: phone video in, browser open, type a phrase, the right object lights up. On my apartment test clip, 7 of 8 natural-language queries ranked the correct object first. The CLI, the pip install, the Colab notebook, and the hosted demo are all shipped.
What’s left for v0.1 is the formal benchmark, LERF and Replica numbers, plus a DA3-vs-VGGT-vs-π³ comparison, which I’m treating as supporting evidence rather than the headline. The claim was never “state of the art.” It’s competitive open-vocabulary querying with zero per-scene optimization, on hardware anyone has.
After that there’s an obvious next layer. The per-object table (positions, embeddings, extents) is exactly the structured context a language model wants. Feed it in and “how many chairs are there?” or “is the mug on the desk?” become answerable. A conversation with a room. That’s Phase 2, and it stays fenced off until v0.1 actually ships, a discipline that has saved this project more than once already.
The point
The foundation models are commodities now; anyone can call them. What’s scarce is the layer above: understanding the geometry well enough to write the glue yourself, making honest engineering trade-offs under real constraints, and packaging the result so a stranger can go from pip install to typing "the red backpack" in under five minutes.
That layer is the actual contribution, and it’s the part no forward pass gives you for free.
Lumen3D is open source (Apache-2.0): code on GitHub. Try the live demo, or build a scene from your own video with the Colab notebook; the free tier is enough.
